_meta.authoringNotes: Definition, autoantigen biology, and clinical features per Pedchenko V et al. NEJM 2010 (PMID 20818345) and McAdoo SP, Pusey CD. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol 2017 review (PMID 28701375). Epidemiology and outcomes per Levy JB et al. Ann Intern Med 2001 (PMID 11700315). Treatment with plasma exchange, corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide per the KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline. Genetic association (HLA-DRB1*15:01) per Phelps RG, Rees AJ. Kidney Int 1999. Rituximab and emerging therapy per recent anti-GBM case series. ANCA double-positivity per McAdoo SP et al. Kidney Int 2017.

heroOverview: Goodpasture syndrome — more precisely called anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease — is a rare, aggressive autoimmune disorder in which the body produces antibodies against type IV collagen in the basement membranes of the kidney and lung. It causes rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, often with simultaneous bleeding into the lungs, a combination known as a pulmonary-renal syndrome. It is rare, with an incidence of roughly 1-2 cases per million people per year, and shows a bimodal age distribution — a peak in young adults (more often men, with both lung and kidney involvement) and a second peak in older adults (more often with kidney-limited disease). Without rapid treatment the disease destroys kidney function within days to weeks and pulmonary hemorrhage can be immediately life-threatening. Goodpasture syndrome is one of the true emergencies of nephrology: outcomes depend critically on how quickly the diagnosis is made and treatment started. Treatment combines plasma exchange to remove the circulating antibody, high-dose corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide to suppress antibody production. With early treatment, lung hemorrhage usually resolves and patient survival is good, but kidney recovery depends heavily on the degree of damage already present at diagnosis — patients who present dialysis-dependent rarely recover kidney function.

keyStats.prevalence: Rare — incidence approximately 1-2 cases per million people per year; accounts for around 1-5% of all glomerulonephritis
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution: a peak in young adults (20-30, more often men, lung and kidney disease) and a second peak in older adults (60-70, more often kidney-limited)
keyStats.avgAge: Two peaks — young adulthood and the seventh decade
keyStats.globalCases: Several hundred to a few thousand new cases per year worldwide; one of the rarest but most aggressive glomerular diseases

definition: Goodpasture syndrome (ICD-10: M31.0), now usually termed anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) disease, is an autoimmune small-vessel disease caused by circulating autoantibodies directed against a specific antigen — the non-collagenous (NC1) domain of the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen — found in the basement membranes of the glomerulus and the pulmonary alveolus. These antibodies bind to the basement membrane and trigger complement activation and an intense inflammatory response, producing crescentic, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis in the kidney and, when the alveolar basement membrane is exposed, bleeding into the lungs (alveolar hemorrhage). The classic full syndrome combines rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with pulmonary hemorrhage — a pulmonary-renal syndrome — but the disease can also present as isolated kidney involvement (anti-GBM glomerulonephritis without lung disease) or, rarely, isolated lung involvement. The term Goodpasture syndrome is sometimes used loosely for any pulmonary-renal syndrome, but Goodpasture disease specifically refers to the anti-GBM antibody-mediated condition. The autoantigen is normally hidden within the structure of the collagen network; disease occurs when it becomes exposed, often after an environmental trigger such as cigarette smoke, hydrocarbon exposure, or respiratory infection, in a person with a genetic predisposition (a strong association with the HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele). A clinically important subgroup — around a third of patients — also has ANCA antibodies (double-positive disease), which behaves somewhat differently and is more prone to relapse. Goodpasture syndrome is managed by nephrology and respiratory medicine, often within an intensive care setting, and is a medical emergency.

typesClassification.description: The full syndrome combining rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with pulmonary hemorrhage. Typically seen in younger adults, more often men, and frequently associated with smoking. The most dramatic and immediately life-threatening presentation.
Anti-GBM antibody-mediated rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis without lung involvement. More common in older adults and a frequent presentation of the disease overall.
Rare presentation with alveolar hemorrhage and anti-GBM antibodies but without overt kidney involvement. Smoking and other inhaled triggers are strongly implicated in lung-predominant disease.

Around a third of patients also have ANCA antibodies. This subgroup is often older, may have a more relapsing course like ANCA-associated vasculitis, and requires consideration of longer-term immunosuppression.
An uncommon variant with anti-GBM antibodies that may not be detected by standard assays, often a more indolent course, and sometimes atypical histology. Requires specialist diagnosis.

primarySymptoms: Coughing up blood (hemoptysis), ranging from blood-streaked sputum to large-volume pulmonary hemorrhage, in patients with lung involvement.
Breathlessness and a dry cough, which can progress rapidly to respiratory failure when alveolar hemorrhage is severe.
Fatigue, malaise, and pallor — often from the anemia that results from blood loss into the lungs and from kidney failure.
Blood in the urine (hematuria), which may be visible or microscopic, reflecting glomerular inflammation.
Reduced or frothy urine output and the swelling and weight gain of fluid retention as kidney function declines.
Rapidly rising blood pressure as the kidneys fail.
Nausea, loss of appetite, and the general symptoms of accumulating kidney waste products (uremia).
Chest tightness or discomfort with the pulmonary hemorrhage.
A relatively short prodrome — many patients are well until days or a few weeks before presentation, when symptoms escalate quickly.
Symptoms of severe anemia — dizziness, palpitations, and marked breathlessness — from combined lung blood loss and kidney failure.
earlyWarningSigns: Coughing up blood, even small amounts, especially in a young adult who smokes
Unexplained breathlessness with a falling hemoglobin level
Blood or protein found in the urine on routine or new testing
A rapidly rising blood creatinine over days to a couple of weeks
New fatigue, breathlessness, and pallor developing together over a short period
emergencySigns: Large-volume hemoptysis or rapidly worsening breathlessness and low oxygen levels — pulmonary hemorrhage is immediately life-threatening and needs emergency care
Rapidly rising creatinine with reduced urine output — rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis is a nephrological emergency
Respiratory failure requiring oxygen, non-invasive ventilation, or intubation
Severe anemia with hemodynamic instability from combined lung and kidney blood loss
Any combination of hemoptysis and acute kidney injury — a pulmonary-renal syndrome must be investigated urgently

causes.cause: Autoantibodies against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen
causes.description: The fundamental cause is the production of autoantibodies targeting the non-collagenous NC1 domain of the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen, a structural protein of the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. These antibodies bind the basement membrane and trigger destructive inflammation.

There is a strong association with the HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele, which influences how the immune system presents the collagen antigen. This genetic background creates susceptibility, on which an environmental trigger acts.

causes.cause: Cigarette smoking and inhaled hydrocarbon exposure
causes.description: Smoking, and exposure to organic solvents and hydrocarbons, damages the alveolar capillary membrane and exposes the normally hidden collagen antigen to the immune system. Smoking is strongly linked to lung involvement and to the classic pulmonary-renal presentation.

causes.cause: Respiratory infection and other lung insults
causes.description: Influenza and other respiratory infections, oxygen toxicity, and pulmonary edema can injure the alveolar membrane and unmask the antigen, helping to explain why lung hemorrhage often accompanies a respiratory trigger.

causes.cause: Prior kidney injury exposing the glomerular antigen
causes.description: Membranous nephropathy, ANCA-associated vasculitis, lithotripsy, urinary obstruction, and other insults to the glomerular basement membrane can precede and possibly trigger anti-GBM disease by exposing the target antigen.

causes.cause: Loss of immune tolerance to a normally hidden self-antigen
causes.description: The unifying mechanism is a breakdown of immune tolerance: the alpha-3 type IV collagen antigen is normally concealed within the collagen network, and disease occurs when, in a genetically predisposed person, it becomes exposed and the immune system mounts an antibody response against it.

riskFactors.description: The strongest genetic association; this HLA allele markedly increases susceptibility to anti-GBM disease by influencing antigen presentation.

Smoking damages the alveolar membrane, exposes the collagen antigen, and is strongly associated with lung involvement and the classic pulmonary-renal syndrome. Smoking cessation is critical at diagnosis.

riskFactors.factor: Inhaled hydrocarbon and organic solvent exposure
riskFactors.description: Occupational or recreational exposure to hydrocarbons and organic solvents is linked to anti-GBM disease, particularly the lung-predominant form.

riskFactors.factor: Young adult age and male sex (classic form)
riskFactors.description: The classic pulmonary-renal syndrome peaks in young men in their twenties; older adults more often have kidney-limited disease with a more even sex distribution.
Influenza and other respiratory infections can act as triggers by injuring the alveolar membrane and exposing the target antigen.
Membranous nephropathy, ANCA-associated vasculitis, lithotripsy, and urinary obstruction can precede anti-GBM disease, possibly by exposing the glomerular antigen.
Around a third of patients are 'double-positive' for ANCA, which influences the clinical course and the need for longer-term immunosuppression.

affectedDemographics: Goodpasture syndrome is rare, with an incidence of roughly 1-2 cases per million people per year.
It shows a bimodal age distribution — a peak in young adults aged 20-30 and a second peak in older adults aged 60-70.
The classic pulmonary-renal form is more common in young men and is strongly linked to smoking; kidney-limited disease in older adults has a more even sex distribution.
It is more common in people of European descent, in keeping with the HLA-DRB1*15:01 association, though it occurs in all populations.
It accounts for around 1-5% of all glomerulonephritis and is one of the leading causes of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis alongside ANCA-associated vasculitis and immune-complex disease.
diagnosisOverview: Goodpasture syndrome is a medical emergency, and the diagnostic process must be rapid because outcomes depend on how quickly treatment is started. It should be suspected in anyone with a pulmonary-renal syndrome — the combination of hemoptysis or alveolar hemorrhage with acute kidney injury — and in anyone with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. The key diagnostic test is detection of circulating anti-GBM antibodies in the blood by enzyme immunoassay, which is highly specific; a positive result in the right clinical context strongly supports the diagnosis and treatment should not wait for confirmatory tests. Because around a third of patients are also ANCA-positive, ANCA testing is performed at the same time, as double-positive disease behaves differently. A kidney biopsy is the confirmatory test and provides prognostic information: it characteristically shows crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear deposition of immunoglobulin along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence, and the proportion of glomeruli affected by crescents and the degree of irreversible scarring predict the chance of renal recovery. Supporting investigations include blood tests showing acute kidney injury, anemia, and inflammation; urinalysis showing hematuria, red cell casts, and proteinuria; and chest imaging (X-ray and CT) showing the diffuse alveolar shadowing of pulmonary hemorrhage. Bronchoscopy and measurement of the carbon monoxide transfer factor (which rises during active alveolar hemorrhage) help confirm lung involvement. The differential diagnosis of a pulmonary-renal syndrome includes ANCA-associated vasculitis, lupus, and other causes, which is why simultaneous testing for anti-GBM antibodies, ANCA, and lupus serologies is standard. The single most important diagnostic principle is a high index of suspicion and urgent testing, because every day of delay in a patient with active disease can mean the difference between kidney recovery and permanent dialysis dependence.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The key diagnostic test — detects circulating antibodies against the glomerular basement membrane; highly specific for the disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, processed urgently. A positive result in a compatible clinical picture confirms the diagnosis and treatment should begin without waiting for the biopsy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the roughly one-third of patients who are 'double-positive', which affects the treatment course and relapse risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn at the same time as the anti-GBM antibody test; results within days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis (crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear immunoglobulin deposition) and assesses the proportion of crescents and scarring to predict renal recovery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound-guided day-case procedure under local anesthesia with several hours of observation afterwards; arranged urgently. Histology guides whether intensive treatment is likely to save kidney function.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal function, full blood count, and inflammatory markers
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the severity of acute kidney injury, the degree of anemia, and the inflammatory response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, often repeated daily; results within hours. Rapidly rising creatinine and falling hemoglobin are characteristic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the hematuria, red cell casts, and proteinuria of active glomerulonephritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urine sample examined with a dipstick and under the microscope; results the same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest imaging (X-ray and CT) and lung assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pulmonary hemorrhage as diffuse alveolar shadowing; bronchoscopy and a raised carbon monoxide transfer factor confirm active alveolar bleeding
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A chest X-ray and CT scan; bronchoscopy if needed to confirm and exclude infection; lung function testing where the patient is stable enough.

treatmentOverview: Goodpasture syndrome is a medical emergency, and treatment must begin urgently — often before the kidney biopsy result is available — because the speed of treatment is the main determinant of outcome. Treatment has three components delivered together. First, plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) physically removes the circulating anti-GBM antibody from the blood; it is performed daily or on alternate days, typically for around two weeks or until antibody levels are no longer detectable, and is the intervention that most rapidly halts ongoing damage, especially to the lungs. Second, high-dose corticosteroids — usually intravenous methylprednisolone pulses followed by oral prednisolone, then tapered over months — suppress the inflammatory response. Third, cyclophosphamide (oral or intravenous) suppresses the production of new antibody by B cells; it is continued for around three months, with rituximab increasingly used as an alternative or in patients who cannot tolerate cyclophosphamide. Alongside this immunosuppressive triad, supportive care is essential: oxygen and, when needed, mechanical ventilation for pulmonary hemorrhage; blood transfusion for anemia; renal replacement therapy (dialysis) for severe kidney failure; treatment and prevention of infection (a major cause of death given the intensive immunosuppression); and absolute smoking cessation, since continued smoking drives ongoing lung hemorrhage. Unlike many autoimmune diseases, anti-GBM disease in most patients is a one-off, monophasic illness — once the antibody is cleared and immunosuppression has been given, it usually does not relapse, so immunosuppression can generally be stopped after about six months. The important exception is double-positive (ANCA-positive) disease, which behaves more like ANCA-associated vasculitis and may need longer maintenance immunosuppression. For patients whose kidneys do not recover and who become dialysis-dependent, kidney transplantation is possible but is generally delayed until anti-GBM antibodies have been undetectable for at least six to twelve months to avoid recurrence in the graft. The overriding clinical message is that early recognition and immediate treatment can be life-saving and kidney-saving, while delay frequently leads to irreversible end-stage kidney disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis)
medicalTreatments.description: Daily or alternate-day removal of plasma to clear circulating anti-GBM antibody, typically for around two weeks or until antibody is undetectable. The fastest way to halt ongoing antibody-mediated damage, particularly pulmonary hemorrhage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapidly controls pulmonary hemorrhage and, combined with immunosuppression, improves renal and patient survival; most effective when started before severe kidney damage is established

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose corticosteroids (intravenous methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Pulsed intravenous methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisolone, tapered over months, to suppress the inflammatory response in the kidney and lung.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: A core component of the immunosuppressive triad; reduces inflammation and supports control of disease activity

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide (oral or intravenous)
medicalTreatments.description: Suppresses production of new anti-GBM antibody by B cells; usually given for around three months. Requires monitoring of blood counts and infection precautions; fertility counseling is important in younger patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with plasma exchange and corticosteroids, prevents ongoing antibody production and is associated with good patient survival in early-treated disease

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab
medicalTreatments.description: A B-cell-depleting monoclonal antibody used as an alternative to cyclophosphamide, particularly in patients who cannot tolerate cyclophosphamide, are younger and wish to preserve fertility, or have double-positive disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increasingly used with good results in case series; standard alternative to cyclophosphamide in selected patients

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (dialysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Hemodialysis supports patients with severe kidney failure during the acute illness. Some patients recover enough kidney function to come off dialysis, while others remain dialysis-dependent depending on the damage at presentation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Life-sustaining supportive treatment; the chance of coming off dialysis depends on how much reversible kidney damage was present at diagnosis

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive respiratory care and blood transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Oxygen, non-invasive ventilation, or intubation for pulmonary hemorrhage and respiratory failure, and red cell transfusion for the anemia caused by lung blood loss and kidney failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential life-support that buys time for the immunosuppressive treatment to control the underlying disease

medicalTreatments.name: Infection prevention and prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Because the treatment is intensively immunosuppressive, infection is a major cause of death; prophylaxis against opportunistic infection (such as Pneumocystis), vaccination where appropriate, and prompt treatment of infection are central.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces treatment-related mortality, which is dominated by infection during intensive immunosuppression

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: An option for patients left with end-stage kidney disease. Transplantation is generally delayed until anti-GBM antibodies have been undetectable for at least 6-12 months to avoid recurrence of the disease in the transplanted kidney.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good long-term graft outcomes when transplantation is delayed until antibodies have cleared; recurrence in the graft is rare under these conditions

surgicalOptions.name: Vascular access surgery for dialysis
surgicalOptions.description: Creation of an arteriovenous fistula or placement of a dialysis catheter for patients who require ongoing renal replacement therapy after the acute illness.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Standard, reliable means of providing long-term dialysis access for patients who remain dialysis-dependent

alternativeTreatments.name: Immunoadsorption
alternativeTreatments.description: A specialized form of antibody removal that selectively adsorbs immunoglobulin from the blood; used at some specialist centres as an alternative to conventional plasma exchange for rapid antibody clearance.

alternativeTreatments.name: Emerging targeted therapies
alternativeTreatments.description: Agents that target the antibody-producing immune response more selectively, including approaches that degrade the pathogenic antibody, are under investigation; none has yet replaced the standard plasma exchange and immunosuppression regimen.

whySeeSpecialist: Goodpasture syndrome is a nephrological and respiratory emergency that must be managed by specialists, usually with intensive care involvement. The combination of hemoptysis with acute kidney injury, or rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis on its own, should prompt immediate referral to nephrology. Specialist centres can deliver urgent anti-GBM antibody testing, kidney biopsy, plasma exchange, and intensive immunosuppression — and the speed of that referral directly affects whether kidney function can be saved.
doctorSelectionGuide: Care should be at a hospital with a nephrology service capable of urgent kidney biopsy, plasma exchange, and renal replacement therapy, with respiratory medicine and intensive care support. Suspected pulmonary-renal syndrome warrants immediate transfer to such a centre. After the acute illness, ongoing follow-up with nephrology monitors kidney function, antibody levels, and — for those who progress to end-stage disease — transplant planning.
hospitalCriteria: Nephrology service with urgent kidney biopsy capability
Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) service available at short notice
Renal replacement therapy and intensive care unit
Respiratory medicine support for pulmonary hemorrhage
Urgent access to anti-GBM antibody and ANCA testing
keyFacilities: Tertiary nephrology centres with plasma exchange
preventionStrategies: Avoid cigarette smoking and stop immediately if diagnosed — smoking drives pulmonary hemorrhage and is the most important modifiable trigger.
Avoid occupational and recreational exposure to organic solvents and hydrocarbons in susceptible individuals.
Seek urgent medical attention for any combination of coughing up blood and reduced or bloody urine — early recognition is the most effective way to prevent irreversible kidney failure.
Ensure rapid investigation of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, since prompt diagnosis and treatment prevent permanent kidney damage.
Once treated, attend follow-up so that the rare relapse — most likely in double-positive disease — is caught early.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking completely and permanently, and avoid second-hand smoke and inhaled hydrocarbon exposure.
Attend all plasma exchange, infusion, and follow-up appointments without interruption during treatment.
Take immunosuppressive medications exactly as prescribed and follow infection-prevention advice.
Report any fever, cough, or sign of infection promptly, as infection is the main treatment-related danger.
Follow renal dietary advice on salt, fluid, potassium, and protein during kidney impairment.
For those left with kidney failure, engage with dialysis and transplant planning and ongoing kidney care.

recommended: A balanced diet adjusted by the renal team for the degree of kidney impairment
Adequate protein and calories to support recovery from a severe acute illness
Iron-rich foods to help recovery from anemia, alongside any prescribed supplementation
Controlled salt intake to help manage blood pressure and fluid balance
avoid: High-potassium foods when blood potassium is raised, as advised by the renal team
High-salt and highly processed foods, which worsen blood pressure and fluid overload
Excess protein during severe kidney impairment unless advised otherwise
Alcohol during intensive immunosuppression, which adds infection and liver risk

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute illness, rest is appropriate and activity is guided by the severity of lung and kidney involvement. As the patient recovers, gentle, gradually increasing physical activity supports reconditioning after a severe illness and intensive treatment. Patients left with chronic kidney disease or on dialysis benefit from individualized exercise advice as part of long-term kidney care.
dailyManagement: Take immunosuppressive and other medications exactly as prescribed.
Follow infection-prevention measures and report fever or new symptoms of infection immediately.
Attend all plasma exchange sessions and monitoring blood tests during the treatment course.
Monitor blood pressure, weight, and urine output as advised by the renal team.
Follow dietary advice on salt, fluid, potassium, and protein during kidney impairment.
Keep all follow-up appointments to monitor kidney function and antibody levels after the acute illness.
prognosis: The prognosis of Goodpasture syndrome has improved dramatically with the modern combination of plasma exchange and immunosuppression, but it remains heavily dependent on how early treatment begins. When treatment is started promptly, patient survival is good — typically over 80-90% at one year — and pulmonary hemorrhage, which used to be the leading cause of early death, usually resolves. The prognosis for the kidneys, however, depends almost entirely on the degree of damage already present at diagnosis. Patients who present with a serum creatinine that is only moderately raised and who are not yet dialysis-dependent have a good chance of recovering useful kidney function. Patients who present already requiring dialysis, or with a very high proportion of glomeruli scarred or affected by crescents on biopsy, rarely recover kidney function and usually progress to permanent end-stage kidney disease requiring long-term dialysis or transplantation. Anti-GBM disease is, for most patients, a monophasic illness — once the antibody is cleared it usually does not return, so immunosuppression can generally be stopped after about six months and long-term relapse is uncommon. The exception is double-positive (ANCA-positive) disease, which behaves more like ANCA-associated vasculitis and carries a higher relapse risk requiring longer follow-up and maintenance immunosuppression. For patients who reach end-stage kidney disease, kidney transplantation has good long-term outcomes provided it is delayed until anti-GBM antibodies have been undetectable for several months. The defining lesson of this disease is that outcomes are decided in the first days — speed of diagnosis and treatment is everything.
recoveryTimeline: Plasma exchange and immunosuppression usually clear circulating anti-GBM antibody within about two weeks. Pulmonary hemorrhage typically settles over days to a couple of weeks with treatment and smoking cessation. Kidney function, when recovery is possible, improves over weeks to a few months; patients who do not recover by then usually remain dialysis-dependent. Immunosuppression is generally continued for around six months in classic monophasic disease. Transplantation, if needed, is delayed until antibodies have been undetectable for 6-12 months.
complications: Life-threatening pulmonary hemorrhage and respiratory failure.
End-stage kidney disease requiring long-term dialysis or transplantation, especially when diagnosis is delayed.
Severe anemia from combined lung and kidney blood loss.
Serious infection during intensive immunosuppression — a major cause of treatment-related death.
Side effects of treatment — bleeding and clotting risks of plasma exchange, the effects of high-dose corticosteroids, and the bone marrow suppression and infertility risk of cyclophosphamide.
Relapse of disease, uncommon in classic anti-GBM disease but more frequent in double-positive (ANCA-positive) disease.
Recurrence of anti-GBM disease in a transplanted kidney if transplantation is performed before antibodies have cleared.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with information on glomerular diseases including anti-GBM disease, kidney failure, dialysis, and transplantation.

supportResources.name: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO)
supportResources.description: International body publishing the clinical practice guideline for glomerular diseases, including anti-GBM disease.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient organisation covering anti-GBM disease and related small-vessel vasculitides, with education and support resources.

supportResources.name: Kidney Care UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient support and information on rare glomerular diseases and kidney failure.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA nephropathy is a much more common, generally slower glomerular disease with mesangial IgA deposition and a chronic course; it rarely causes pulmonary hemorrhage. Goodpasture syndrome is rare, aggressive, causes rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis often with lung hemorrhage, and shows linear (not mesangial) antibody deposition with positive anti-GBM antibodies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus nephritis is kidney involvement in systemic lupus, with positive ANA and lupus-specific antibodies, low complement, and immune-complex (granular) deposition on biopsy, usually with other features of lupus. Goodpasture syndrome has anti-GBM antibodies and linear deposition and is typically not part of a multisystem autoimmune disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nephritic Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nephritic syndrome describes the clinical pattern of hematuria, red cell casts, hypertension, and reduced kidney function from glomerular inflammation. Goodpasture syndrome is one specific, anti-GBM antibody-mediated cause of a rapidly progressive nephritic picture, identified by antibody testing and biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Kidney Injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute kidney injury is a broad category of sudden loss of kidney function from many causes. Goodpasture syndrome is one specific, immune-mediated cause of acute kidney injury through rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, and recognizing it matters because it needs urgent, specific immunosuppressive treatment rather than supportive care alone.

relatedConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another antibody-related glomerular disease; part of the differential for hematuria and glomerulonephritis.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: A key differential for a pulmonary-renal syndrome and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

relatedConditions.name: Nephritic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Goodpasture syndrome is a specific cause of a rapidly progressive nephritic presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Kidney Injury
relatedConditions.relevance: Goodpasture syndrome is an important treatable cause of acute kidney injury from rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

relatedConditions.name: End stage renal disease
relatedConditions.relevance: A frequent outcome when diagnosis is delayed and kidney damage is already irreversible at presentation.

faqs.question: What is Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: Goodpasture syndrome, or anti-GBM disease, is a rare autoimmune condition in which antibodies attack type IV collagen in the basement membranes of the kidney and lung. It causes rapidly progressive kidney failure, often with bleeding into the lungs, and is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What causes Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: It is caused by autoantibodies against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen. Disease occurs in genetically predisposed people (strongly linked to the HLA-DRB1*15:01 gene) when an environmental trigger — cigarette smoke, inhaled hydrocarbons, or respiratory infection — exposes the normally hidden collagen antigen to the immune system.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: Lung involvement causes coughing up blood, breathlessness, and fatigue from blood loss. Kidney involvement causes blood in the urine, reduced urine output, swelling, high blood pressure, and the symptoms of kidney failure. The combination of coughing up blood with kidney failure is the classic presentation.

faqs.question: How is Goodpasture syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The key test is a blood test for anti-GBM antibodies, which is highly specific. ANCA testing is done at the same time because a third of patients have both. A kidney biopsy confirms the diagnosis, showing crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear antibody deposition, and predicts the chance of kidney recovery.

faqs.question: Is Goodpasture syndrome an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is one of the true emergencies of kidney medicine. Pulmonary hemorrhage can be immediately life-threatening, and the kidneys can be destroyed within days to weeks. Outcomes depend critically on how quickly the diagnosis is made and treatment started.

faqs.question: How is Goodpasture syndrome treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines three things: plasma exchange to remove the harmful antibody from the blood, high-dose corticosteroids to suppress inflammation, and cyclophosphamide or rituximab to stop new antibody being produced. Supportive care includes oxygen, blood transfusion, dialysis, and infection prevention.

faqs.question: Can Goodpasture syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: For most patients it is a one-off, monophasic illness — once the antibody is cleared and treatment given, it usually does not return, so immunosuppression can often be stopped after about six months. In this sense it can be effectively cured, although kidney damage already done is often permanent.

faqs.question: Will my kidneys recover after Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: It depends on how much damage was present at diagnosis. Patients treated early, before they need dialysis, have a good chance of recovering kidney function. Patients who are already dialysis-dependent at presentation, or who have extensive scarring on biopsy, rarely recover kidney function.

faqs.question: Why is smoking dangerous in Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: Cigarette smoke damages the lining of the lung's air sacs and exposes the collagen antigen that the antibodies attack. Smoking is strongly linked to lung hemorrhage and drives ongoing bleeding, so stopping smoking immediately and completely is a critical part of treatment.

faqs.question: What is anti-GBM disease?
faqs.answer: Anti-GBM disease is the precise medical name for Goodpasture syndrome. GBM stands for glomerular basement membrane, the kidney structure that the antibodies attack. The same antibodies also attack the lung's basement membrane, which is why kidney and lung disease occur together.

faqs.question: Is Goodpasture syndrome hereditary?
faqs.answer: It is not directly inherited, but there is a strong genetic predisposition through the HLA-DRB1*15:01 gene, which makes the immune system more likely to react against the collagen antigen. Disease occurs when this predisposition is combined with an environmental trigger such as smoking or infection.

faqs.question: What is plasma exchange and why is it used?
faqs.answer: Plasma exchange, or plasmapheresis, is a procedure that filters the blood to physically remove the harmful anti-GBM antibodies. It is done daily or on alternate days for about two weeks and is the fastest way to stop ongoing antibody damage, particularly to the lungs.

faqs.question: Can Goodpasture syndrome come back?
faqs.answer: In classic anti-GBM disease, relapse is uncommon — it is usually a one-time illness. However, the roughly one-third of patients who also have ANCA antibodies (double-positive disease) behave more like ANCA-associated vasculitis and have a higher relapse risk, needing longer follow-up and maintenance treatment.

faqs.question: Can you have a kidney transplant after Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients who develop end-stage kidney disease can be transplanted, but the transplant is generally delayed until anti-GBM antibodies have been undetectable for at least 6-12 months. Under these conditions, recurrence of the disease in the transplanted kidney is rare and long-term outcomes are good.

faqs.question: Who gets Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: It is rare, affecting about 1-2 people per million per year. It has two age peaks — young adults in their twenties, more often men with the classic lung-and-kidney form, and older adults in their sixties to seventies, more often with kidney-limited disease. Smoking strongly increases the risk of the lung form.

faqs.question: What is a pulmonary-renal syndrome?
faqs.answer: A pulmonary-renal syndrome is the combination of bleeding into the lungs with rapidly progressive kidney inflammation. Goodpasture syndrome is one important cause; others include ANCA-associated vasculitis and lupus. Any patient with coughing up blood plus acute kidney injury needs urgent investigation for these conditions.

faqs.question: How quickly does Goodpasture syndrome damage the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Very quickly. Untreated, anti-GBM disease can destroy kidney function within days to a few weeks through rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. This is why it is treated as an emergency and why every day of delay in diagnosis reduces the chance of saving kidney function.

faqs.question: What is the main danger during Goodpasture syndrome treatment?
faqs.answer: Infection is the main treatment-related danger. The combination of plasma exchange, high-dose corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide is intensively immunosuppressive, and serious infection is a leading cause of death. Infection prevention, prophylaxis, and prompt treatment of any infection are central to care.

faqs.question: Does Goodpasture syndrome always affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: No. The classic form affects both lungs and kidneys, but the disease can also present as kidney-limited anti-GBM glomerulonephritis without lung involvement, which is more common in older adults. Isolated lung involvement without kidney disease is rare and strongly linked to smoking.

faqs.question: What is double-positive Goodpasture syndrome?
faqs.answer: Around a third of patients with anti-GBM disease also have ANCA antibodies — this is called double-positive disease. These patients are often older, may have a more relapsing course like ANCA-associated vasculitis, and generally need longer-term maintenance immunosuppression and follow-up than patients with classic anti-GBM disease.

symptomKeywords: coughing up blood and kidney failure
metaDescription: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease): autoimmune attack on kidney and lung. Symptoms, urgent diagnosis, plasma exchange and immunosuppression reviewed.
